data.title: 'We are old school': I've been married for 40 years. Should I have kept my money separate?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'We have all of our financial accounts joint except for our IRAs'

"Times have changed." (Photo subject is a model.)

Dear Quentin,

Times have changed. If you are married, do you keep your money separate from your spouse's? I have been married over 40 years, and we are old school. We have all of our financial accounts joint except for our IRAs.

Happily Married

Related: I'm trying to fix my relationship with my stepdaughter. Should my husband and I tell her how we have divided our assets?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com. The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

Times are changing, but more slowly than you might imagine.

Dear Happy,

People are increasingly savvy about curating their finances, especially when they get hitched.

Young people building a life together may be more likely to have joint bank accounts if they both have little to nothing saved yet. They'll be more likely to achieve their financial goals by sharing expenses and investments.

That doesn't include workplace retirement accounts like 401(k)s and IRAs taken out separately. Nor, I imagine, would it include real estate that one spouse owned before they wed. You and your husband probably started out on an equal footing.

If I had a banana for every letter I received about a spouse behaving recklessly with a couple's finances, or acting in some kind of underhanded way during a divorce, I'd be able to open a large fruit store (selling just bananas).

Times are changing, but more slowly than you might imagine. Nearly a quarter of married couples have separate accounts, up from 15% 30 years ago, according to the latest figures from the U.S. Census Bureau.

Separate accounts and prenuptial agreements are just another form of insurance.

But while they are still the majority, the share of those with joint bank accounts declined to roughly 77% of married, cohabiting couples. That includes checking, savings and money-market accounts and CDs, and is down from 85% in 1996.

One reason for this is that people are marrying later and often have more assets when they do marry, such as stocks, real estate and savings, and they are more reluctant to merge their finances should the marriage end up dissolving - their money along with it.

Thirty years ago, the median age at first marriage was roughly 28 for women and 27 for men. These ages have climbed to 29 for women and 30 for men, according to the Current Population Survey.

With my advice-giving cap on, allow me to say that it's better for a couple to have these conversations up front. Will they share expenses 50/50? What happens if one person owns their home and the other one rents? Will they contribute to the mortgage?

And, to your point, do they have a joint account for expenses? A prenuptial agreement? Things can go south very quickly if they start having money troubles due to job loss or if one spouse has an issue with substance misuse or gambling.

Moving the goalposts

One woman wrote to me a few years ago to say her husband had opened a new bank account and was depositing his paychecks there. He made $150,000 a year and she made $45,000.

"He asked me to pay $900 a month toward our $3,000 rent," she said. "Is this fair? I bring home $2,400 per month. I feel slighted. I guess I also feel bitter because I never felt comfortable paying for his kids. What do you think?"

Fairness is relative. Given that she was living rent-free and it was a second marriage, it was hard to argue that it was fair or unfair. It was also difficult to make a case that she should not pay rent. "This should be a negotiation," I told her, "not a fait accompli."

And then there are the outliers, like this woman whose husband wants to leave her everything, even risking the ire of his children. "In later-in-life marriages, is it typical for an older husband to leave everything to his younger wife?" she asked me.

The short answer is no. Usually wives and husbands divide their separate property among their heirs and their second spouse, but there's no rule that says they must do that. Some states do have laws that require an "elective share" be left to a spouse.

Certain jurisdictions may give the surviving spouse a right of up to 50% of the value of their marital property.

An elective share refers to the minimum amount that a spouse may inherit in the absence of prenup or postnup. Certain jurisdictions may give the surviving spouse a right of up to 50% of the value of the marital-property portion of the augmented estate.

A few members of the Moneyist Facebook Group (META) weighed in on your question. One man wrote: "When my mom died and I received a generous inheritance, it was kept separate and in my name only, as suggested by our lawyer."

"I still consider it 'our' money," he added. "It has been used to help our kids, pay off our house and purchase an investment property, all of which has commingled some of it." Of course, he can spend it as he wants. And the remainder belongs to him.

"When my mother-in-law dies, any inheritance there will be put in just my wife's name as well," he added. "We have no intention of divorce (30 years married) but you never know. All other money is and has been maintained in joint accounts."

You have 40 years of happy marriage. You know each other pretty well. You are probably financially stable at this point in your lives. Joint accounts work for you. That doesn't make sense for everyone. Separate accounts and prenups are just another form of insurance.

Related: 'We keep our finances separate': My boyfriend is in his 50s with no retirement savings - how worried should I be?

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

'I plan to exit corporate life': I'm 50 and have $400,000. My wife is a teacher. Can I retire at 55?

'This is an overlooked catastrophe': Why do so many hospitals not accept Medicare Advantage for cancer patients?

We're in our 70s with no heirs. I like donating $30,000 from our $700,000 IRA to charity - my husband disagrees. Who's right?

'I was shoveling sidewalks at 8 years old': I'm a 73-year-old boomer dad with two kids. Here's what I teach them about finance

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-30-26 1122ET

data.title: Target-date retirement funds are more popular than ever. Critics say you can do better.
data.description:
By Venessa Wong

Your 401(k) is probably in a target-date fund. This simple hack may help you earn more.

Target-date funds provide a simple, hands-off approach to investing for retirement. What people should avoid, according to one financial adviser, is putting money into a target-date fund at age 22 and not thinking about it for the next 40 years.

Join MarketWatch for a live talk about teen investing on Wednesday, May 6 at 1:30 p.m. Eastern time. Sign up and submit your questions for the Q&A here.

To Jesse Cramer, target-date funds, which automatically rebalance to gradually become more conservative as the account holder's planned year of retirement - the fund's "target date" - approaches, are a well-meaning but flawed tool for the growing number of investors who are using them.

"If you have to park your money" for a period of time in a low-cost, passively managed target-date fund "while you get more confident and learn about how to invest, to me, that is a great outcome," Cramer said.

What people should avoid, however, is putting money into a target-date fund at age 22 and not thinking about it "for the next 40 years," the financial adviser and host of the "Personal Finance for Long-Term Investors" podcast told MarketWatch.

But that is exactly how target-date retirement funds are intended to work, and that's supposed to be the attraction: The investor makes one choice - the year they will retire - and then sets it and forgets it. Target-date funds provide an option for people who do not wish to actively manage their retirement account, who have little knowledge of finance or who may struggle to invest for retirement at all. They also are often the default option when employers automatically enroll workers into a 401(k).

The simplicity has proved very appealing. There was $4.8 trillion in target-date assets in 2025, up 20% year over year, according to Morningstar. Large players in the target-date retirement market include Vanguard, Fidelity and Capital Group.

Retirement news on MarketWatch: Trump to sign order creating retirement plans for workers who lack them

Investing for retirement is a challenge for many Americans: About 44.1% of people with full-time jobs (excluding government and self-employed workers) do not participate in their employer's retirement plan, according to an analysis of U.S. Census data. Among those who do participate, many wish they had more guidance on how to invest their retirement assets. So when offered the option of a target-date fund, 84% of participants use it, according to Vanguard data.

Research has shown that when a target-date fund is the default investment option, younger enrollees have a higher share invested in stocks compared with those who did not have a target-date option, according to a report by MIT Sloan.

Despite these advances, some financial professionals say target-date funds could still do better. Even as the funds have shifted over the years to include a larger share of stocks, target-date "glide paths" - or the rate at which the fund rebalances - add bonds into the mix way too soon, they argue, forgoing too much stock-market growth when investors are still decades away from retiring and when returns on bonds have been low. This can lead to a shortfall in a longer retirement, particularly as life expectancy in the U.S. reached a record high of 79 years in 2024.

Related: A long life isn't cheap: Underestimating your longevity can be very expensive

Vanguard's target-date fund for those planning to retire in 2040, for instance, already has 25% in bonds and is designed to gradually ramp up to 50% by age 65 and to 70% by age 72.

This strategy is supposed to balance risks such as market volatility, longevity and inflation over a long period of time.

"Maintaining meaningful equity exposure near and into retirement helps address longevity risk and supports long-term purchasing power, while the final landing point is aligned with the period when investors are typically drawing down assets to fund retirement spending," Brian Miller, Vanguard's head of multi-asset product management, told MarketWatch.

To adjust to changing needs and offer more growth potential, many target-date funds have gradually increased their equity allocation over the last decade, according to Morningstar, with the median equity exposure for people 45 years from retirement at 93% in 2025, up from 89% a decade earlier.

Fidelity recently updated its glide path to have higher equity exposure for both early-career investors and those already in retirement. "Fidelity's target-date investment team believes that higher equity exposure has potential to improve the portfolios' ability to support income in retirement. In addition, increased exposure to inflation-sensitive assets [like Treasury inflation-protected securities] may improve portfolio resiliency during inflationary environments," a spokesperson told MarketWatch.

Cramer, who is 36, still sees this glide path as potentially too conservative. "I'm pretty comfortable putting a lot of money in the stock market" until retirement is about 10 to 15 years away, he told MarketWatch.

Some target-date funds also have high fees, as Cramer talked about in a recent episode of his show. While expense ratios for these funds have declined by half in the past decade, according to Morningstar, they still average 0.27%. Vanguard's target-date retirement funds, which lead the industry, charge 0.08%, which is an acceptable rate, he said, but others charge much more.

If the fee is above 0.15%, "at least ask the question of what's going on," he said. "Once you get into 40, 50, 60 basis points, that's where you just say, 'OK, this is high. Why is this high?'"

"Target-date funds really come down to simplicity versus optimization," Michael Bogardus, a financial planner at Barnum Financial Group, told MarketWatch. "They solve a real problem by giving people a diversified portfolio that automatically adjusts risk tolerance over time, but that convenience often comes with lower long-term growth potential."

Read more: This type of 401(k) plan could boost retirement savings up to 22% - but it comes at a price

A simple workaround

For those who feel the fund for their retirement year may not sufficiently capture stock-market gains, there is a simple workaround. J.L. Collins, author of "The Simple Path to Wealth," wrote this on his blog: "If you want more aggressive (greater percentage of stocks), just pick a later date." For instance, if you plan to retire in 2040, select a fund for a later year, making sure that the glide path aligns with your preferences.

"If you want a more conservative (greater percentage of bonds) approach," he wrote, "choose a date before your actual retirement. The earlier the date the more conservative the asset allocation."

Collins recommended target-date retirement funds, calling them in his post "an excellent choice for many, maybe most people," but he noted that he still has "a slight preference" for the "simple" strategy of holding Vanguard's Total Stock Market Index VTSAX and adding bonds (specifically, Vanguard's Total Bond Market Index Fund ) when a person retires and starts living off their investments.

Manage your own retirement portfolio

With some clear guidelines and a little extra effort, some experts say it's possible for 401(k) investors to do better than target-date retirement funds and potentially retire with more money. If their plan allows, they can choose their own investments instead, focusing on lower-cost options and a more stock-heavy mix, increasing bond exposure only when they are close to retirement. Trading within tax-advantaged retirement accounts like 401(k)s and IRAs doesn't incur capital-gains taxes, so swapping out one investment for another can be done easily.

Doing this, however, does require a clear understanding of your financial needs and your risk tolerance. Someone who needs to catch up on retirement savings, for example, may need to stay aggressively invested as long as possible to grow their nest egg faster, Cramer said, while a person who is expecting a windfall may want to keep their retirement portfolio in stocks but invest that windfall more conservatively if those funds will be used first.

Cramer invested his 401(k) into three funds: a total U.S. stock index fund (50%), a total international index fund (40%) and a total U.S. bond index fund (10%).

Although he is not close to retiring, Cramer still does not want 100% exposure to stocks, and his allocation is actually not dramatically different from target-date funds for people his age. The difference is that he will only start ramping up bond exposure 10 to 15 years before retiring. By comparison, he said, "the average target-date fund will start gliding your money down [to hold more bonds] over 25 years."

Target-date retirement funds are a reasonable default, Bogardus said, but they "can also cause investors to miss opportunities to personalize their strategy around their specific income needs as retirement approaches." An investor's aim is for a portfolio to "generate the growth and income needed to support someone for the rest of their life," but each person's path to getting there will be different. "That is well beyond what a target-date fund is designed to do," he said.

What personal-finance issues would you like to see covered in MarketWatch? We would like to hear from readers about their financial decisions and money-related questions. You can write to us at readerstories@marketwatch.com. A reporter may be in touch to learn more. MarketWatch will not attribute your answers to you by name without your permission.

-Venessa Wong

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-30-26 1125ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Financial Services Headlines at 11 AM ET: Europe's Economy Stutters as War Drives Up Inflation | Mastercard ...
data.description:
Europe's Economy Stutters as War Drives Up Inflation

Both the ECB and BOE, facing a mix of weaker growth and higher inflation, held interest rates steady.

----

Mastercard Profit, Sales Rise as Payment Network Arm Grows

The credit-card company posted a profit of $3.88 billion, or $4.35 a share, in the quarter ended March 31, compared with $3.28 billion, or $3.59 a share, a year earlier.

----

Blue Owl Distributable Earnings Rise, Revenue Climbs

Blue Owl Capital's first-quarter distributable earnings climbed as the asset manager logged higher revenue and grew its assets under management.

----

Blackstone Adds New Unit to Focus Its AI Momentum

Called BXN1, the new arm will concentrate some of the firm's AI bets as it also underscores Stephen Schwarzman's conviction in the technology.

----

Powell Won't Leave. The Fed Won't Cut. Warsh Will Have to Deal With Both.

A divided committee, external political pressures, and an energy shock-Kevin Warsh is inheriting a Fed unlike any his predecessors faced.

----

GTCR Hunts for Initial Acquisition With Newly Launched Data Analytics Firm

Former OPIS Chief Executive Brian Crotty will lead the new business.

----

BNP Paribas Shares Slide as Heavier Provisions Take Shine Off Earnings Beat

BNP Paribas slipped after the French lender set aside more than $1 billion in provisions, overshadowing a first-quarter profit beat.

----

Spain's BBVA Posts Earnings Beat as Sustained Loan Growth Cushions

Banco Bilbao Vizcaya Argentaria reported a rise in profit on continued lending growth and a steady performance in its largest markets.

----

DCC Rejects $6.7 billion KKR Consortium Bid

The company said the takeover approach from private-equity firms KKR and Energy Capital Partners undervalues it and its future prospects.

----

Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on ING Groep, BNP Paribas, BBVA and more in the latest Market Talks covering Financial Services.

----

EBay First-Quarter Sales Rise, Helped by Strength in Collectibles

The company posted a first-quarter profit of $512 million as its gross merchandise volume rose 18%.

----

Standard Chartered's Profit Jumps, Books $190 Million Charge on Iran Conflict

The London-based bank said higher income from investment and insurance products boosted profit at its wealth and retail banking business. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 30, 2026 11:15 ET (15:15 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Dow Jones Top Energy Headlines at 11 AM ET: Oil Prices Slide After Spiking to Four-Year High. Why Iran War Fears Are Growing. | U.S. ...
data.description:
Oil Prices Slide After Spiking to Four-Year High. Why Iran War Fears Are Growing.

Front-month international crude prices hit a wartime high as investors worry that the U.S. could restart its attacks on Iran.

----

U.S. Natural Gas Futures Steady Ahead of Storage Data

U.S. natural gas futures remained in a holding pattern with little change in overnight weather forecasts.

----

ConocoPhillips Cuts Outlook, Logs Lower Earnings

ConocoPhillips reported a first-quarter profit of $2.18 billion, or $1.78 a share, down from a year earlier, amid lower gas prices in Permian and lower volumes.

----

Repsol's Earnings Rise as Traders Get Boost From Iran Conflict

Spain's Repsol posted a jump in first-quarter profit as its refineries and traders capitalized on disruption to global energy markets triggered by conflict in the Middle East.

----

OMV's Top Line Falls on Lower-Gas Prices and Oil Sales

Revenue was hit by lower-gas prices and a drop in oil sales, but higher refining margins in its chemicals business offered some support.

----

Octopus Energy Is Getting Into the Carbon Credit Business

British renewable-power utility said it is seeing growth in companies looking to find ways to offset rising emissions.

----

The U.A.E.'s OPEC Bombshell Signals a New Middle East Order

After bearing the brunt of Iran's counterattack, the financial powerhouse is strengthening ties with Israel and challenging Saudi Arabia.

----

Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on OMV, Repsol, Woodside Energy and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities.

----

Shark Tank's Mr. Wonderful Is Planning One of America's Biggest Data Centers

Kevin O'Leary said he and his local partners in Utah are looking for county approval to build a 40,000-acre project 'in a competition with the Chinese.'

----

TotalEnergies Increases Shareholder Returns as Iran Conflict Boosts Earnings

TotalEnergies said it would return more cash to shareholders as the conflict in the Middle East provides an earnings windfall.

----

U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall on Record Weekly Exports

Crude oil stockpiles fell by 6.2 million barrels, much larger than the 100,000 decline analysts expected, as weekly exports rose by 1.6 million barrels a day, U.S. government data showed.

----

The Global Energy Order Is Breaking Down

The Iran war is accelerating a shift from an oil market structured around economic efficiency toward one shaped by politics and conflict.

----

U.A.E.'s OPEC Exit Deals Major Blow to Cartel Amid Middle East Oil Squeeze

The Persian Gulf state said the move would help it meet changing demand but analysts see it as a heavy blow to the organization. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 30, 2026 11:15 ET (15:15 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: The U.A.E.'s OPEC Bombshell Signals a New Middle East Order — Commodities Roundup
data.description:
MARKET MOVEMENTS:

--Brent crude oil is down 2.9% to $114.56 a barrel.

--European benchmark gas is down 1.4% to 46.22 euros a megawatt-hour.

--Copper futures are down 0.1% to $12,969.50 a metric ton.

--Gold futures are up 1.6% at $4,631.60 a troy ounce. 




  TOP STORY: 



  The U.A.E.'s OPEC Bombshell Signals a New Middle East Order 



  The United Arab Emirates' decision to withdraw from OPEC did more than deliver a shock to the cartel that has long ruled the global oil market. It also rang the opening bell for the new geopolitical order that the war with Iran is ushering in across the Middle East. 



  The new alignment is redrawing political fault lines between the Arab world and Israel that defined the region for decades. Instead, the U.A.E.--the financial nerve center of the Gulf and a potent military power--is strengthening security cooperation with Israel as both states work to change the region's strategic balance through force of arms if necessary. 



  Israel recently sent its Iron Dome missile-defense technology and troops to operate it to the Gulf state, people familiar with the matter said, something that would be unthinkable elsewhere in the Arab world. 





  OTHER STORIES: 



  Endeavour Mining Has Avoided Surging Fuel Costs Amid Iran War, CEO Says -- Interview 



  Endeavour Mining has so far avoided a surge in fuel costs despite the conflict in the Middle East pushing crude oil prices well above $100 a barrel. 



  War-induced cost pressures have been relatively muted so far, even as fuel accounts for around 15% of the gold miner's expenses, Chief Executive Officer Ian Cockerill said in an interview. 



  -- 



  Mara to Buy Long Ridge Energy & Power in $1.5 Billion Deal 



  Mara agreed to acquire Long Ridge Energy & Power for $1.52 billion. 



  The Miami-based energy infrastructure company said Thursday the deal would include Long Ridge's 505-megawatt gas power plant in Ohio, as well as 1,600 acres supporting a digital infrastructure campus. 



  -- 



  OGE Energy to Power Three Google Data Centers in Oklahoma 



  OGE Energy signed a deal to power three data centers for Alphabet's Google. 



  The Oklahoma City energy company will service new data centers that the tech conglomerate announced in Muskogee and Stillwater, Okla., OGE said Thursday. 



  -- 



  Octopus Energy Is Getting Into the Carbon Credit Business 



  Octopus Energy, the British utility known for bringing renewable energy to U.K. and European households, is betting big on U.S. carbon removals, investing more than half a billion dollars in a California startup just as the market appears to be slowing. 



  The Generation arm of the London-based clean-tech unicorn is taking a $13 million equity stake in Living Carbon, which aims to reforest degraded land across Ohio, Pennsylvania and West Virginia. On top of that, Octopus is making a $500 million investment in its projects, to ensure the startup can deliver the carbon credits it is promising. 



  -- 



  Iran War Boosts a Fast-Growing Chinese Industry: Turning Coal Into Stuff 



  The historic spree of coal burning that powered China's economic rise is finally leveling off. But the fossil fuel does have other uses-and war in Iran is giving one in particular a big boost. 



  The conflict has been calamitous for much of Asia's petrochemicals industry, which largely relies on oil from the Middle East to make ingredients for everything from PVC pipes to paracetamol. The effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz has meant higher costs and, in some cases, shortages. 



  -- 



  Investors Come to Terms With a New Reality of Expensive Oil 



  "Higher for longer" isn't just a rates story anymore. Oil prices are now joining in, too. 



  Investors this week have started to reckon with the possibility that oil prices may stay elevated for longer than expected, as peace talks between the U.S. and Iran show no signs of progress. 



  -- 



  ConocoPhillips Cuts Outlook, Logs Lower 1Q Earnings as Iran War Disrupts Operations 



  ConocoPhillips lowered its production outlook for the year due to uncertainty surrounding the war in the Middle East. 



  The Houston-based oil producer Thursday forecast production of 2.3 million to 2.33 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day, down from a prior outlook of 2.33 million to 2.36 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day. 



  -- 



  Valero Swings to 1Q Profit on Higher Revenue, Refining Margins 



  Valero Energy swung to a first-quarter profit as the spike in oil and gasoline prices caused by the Iran war bolstered refining margins, a trend Valero expects to continue. 



  The San Antonio oil refiner posted earnings of $1.26 billion, or $4.22 a share, in contrast to a loss of $595 million, or $1.90 a share, a year earlier, and eclipsing the average Wall Street target of $3.12 a share. 



  -- 



  Oil Rallies to Highest Level Since Start of Iran War 



  Oil prices surged to a wartime high on fears that the U.S. might resume military action against Iran in order to break deadlocked peace negotiations, threatening to prolong supply disruptions and intensify the global energy shock. 



  In early European trading, Brent crude for June delivery, which expires on Thursday, rose 4.1% to $122.82 a barrel after surpassing $123, its highest level since 2022. The more active July contract was up 2.1% to $112.70 a barrel. West Texas Intermediate futures for June gained 1.6% to $108.56 a barrel. 



  -- 



  Repsol's Earnings Rise as Traders Get Boost From Iran Conflict 



  Spain's Repsol posted a jump in first-quarter profit as its refineries and traders capitalized on disruption to global energy markets triggered by conflict in the Middle East. 



  Repsol's adjusted net income jumped by over 50% to 873 million euros ($1.02 billion). It missed the 897 million euros analysts had expected, according to a company-compiled consensus. 





  MARKET TALKS: 



  U.S. Natural Gas Futures Steady Ahead of Storage Data -- Market Talk 



  0956 ET - U.S. natural gas futures remain in a holding pattern with little change in overnight weather forecasts. Market focus is on the EIA weekly inventory report, which is expected to show a larger-than-usual injection expanding the surplus over the five-year average. "On the heels of three straight bearish EIA readings, a surprise in either direction may confirm loose supply/demand balances or spur a price rebound," Eli Rubin of EBW Analytics says in a note. The EIA report is due at 10:30 a.m. ET. Analysts in a WSJ survey predict a 79 Bcf injection. Nymex natural gas is up 0.3% at $2.656/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Oil Futures Pull Back In Volatile Trade on Middle East Uncertainty -- Market Talk 



  0942 ET - Oil futures are lower in early U.S. trade, retreating from their overnight surge with the market uncertain about the next developments in the Middle East. "While the deadlock continues, the Straight of Hormuz remains closed, and both WTI and Brent crude futures seem to be pricing in a longer term of tighter supplies," Dennis Kissler of BOK Financial says in a note. "Still, any sign of a peace deal or opening of the strait could trigger a massive long liquidation sell-off." Brent for June delivery is down 3.2% at $114.26 a barrel ahead of today's expiration, after soaring to a four-year-high overnight. WTI is off 1.6% at $105.15 barrel.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  Oil Futures Retreat After Rising to Four-Year Highs -- Market Talk 



  1219 GMT - Oil prices pull back after climbing to a four-year high earlier on persistent concerns over a prolonged conflict between the U.S. and Iran and a deepening energy shock. In afternoon European trading, Brent crude for June delivery is down 3.4% to $114.07 a barrel after rising above $126 a barrel earlier in the session and reaching its highest level since 2022. The July contract--which becomes the front-month on Friday--is down 1.3% to $108.50 a barrel. The drop appears driven more by broader market volatility than by any clear new catalyst. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  U.A.E. Exit From OPEC Expected to Fuel Global Oil Competition -- Market Talk 



  1153 GMT - The United Arab Emirates could increase production independently and compete more aggressively for global market share after leaving OPEC and OPEC+. "Any production cuts by OPEC to support prices would create opportunities for producers outside the cartel to ramp up their own output and gain market share," says Thu Lan Nguyen from Commerzbank. The U.A.E.'s departure is set to reduce OPEC+'s market share from roughly 48% toward about 44%, weakening the group's overall influence. With significant spare capacity--estimated between 700,000 and over 1 million barrels per day, according to Commerzbank--the U.A.E. is well positioned to expand output quickly, also diminishing OPEC's ability to manage and stabilize prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  ConocoPhillips' Operations Disrupted by Iran War -- Market Talk 



  0953 ET - ConocoPhillips says it is excluding Qatar from its production guidance for the current quarter, citing uncertainty around the war in the Middle East. The oil producer also cuts its full-year production outlook to account for the exclusion, now calling for production of 2.3 million to 2.33 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day this year. That compares with a prior outlook of 2.33 million to 2.36 million barrels of oil-equivalent per day. ConocoPhillips is off 1.8% in early trading after reporting lower 1Q earnings. (connor.hart@wsj.com) 



  -- 



  European Gas Prices Below 50 Euros On Seasonally Weaker Demand -- Market Talk 



  (MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 30, 2026 11:04 ET (15:04 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Big Tech's $700 billion spending on AI this year is called the 'greatest capital misallocation in history'
data.description:
By Christine Ji

Alphabet, Amazon, Meta and Microsoft are depleting their cash reserves and raising debt as AI spending moves even higher

Amazon's free cash flow fell to $1.2 billion in the first quarter, a 95% drop year-over-year.

Investors were already uneasy last quarter when Alphabet, Amazon.com, Meta Platforms and Microsoft announced plans to spend a cumulative $650 billion on artificial intelligence in 2026. So when three of the four hyperscalers shared plans to spend even more on Wednesday, those concerns were amplified.

Now, the anticipated capital expenditures among these Big Tech companies is closer to $700 billion: $190 billion for Microsoft (MSFT), between $180 and $190 billion for Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), between $125 billion and $145 billion for Meta (META) and $200 billion for Amazon (AMZN), which was the only company to keep its forecast intact.

Gary Marcus, an entrepreneur and prominent AI researcher, called Big Tech's spending the "greatest capital misallocation in history" in an X post following Wednesday's earnings announcements.

"None are making major profits on AI; none has a technical moat; a massive price war is inevitable. And few of their customers are seeing major returns on investment," Marcus wrote.

Read: Why Alphabet's stock is the standout gainer on Big Tech's monster earnings day

Essentially, the hyperscalers are pouring money into large language models that can be commoditized and difficult to monetize, he said.

Alphabet, Amazon, Meta and Microsoft all mentioned being capacity-constrained on their earnings calls, which is why they're racing to spend billions to bring data centers and chips online. Additionally, William Blair analyst Dylan Carden said out in a Thursday note that supply-chain shortages with memory chips have led to price increases, further elevating spend.

While Wall Street analysts haven't written off the entire AI trade, as Marcus has, they've become more discerning about winners and losers. Big Tech companies must show revenue acceleration and cost discipline in other areas as elevated capex levels put pressure on free cash flow.

Three months ago, analysts believed Amazon was on track to become the first hyperscaler with negative free cash flows in 2026. Yesterday's results showed that Amazon's first-quarter free cash flow plunged 95% year-over-year to $1.2 billion. Alphabet's free cash flow dropped 47% to $10.1 billion. However, JPMorgan analyst Doug Anmuth wrote on Thursday that both companies showed strong growth in cloud backlog, which justified the spend.

Declining free-cash-flow levels have led Big Tech companies to take on debt. On Thursday, Meta announced plans for a six-part investment-grade bond issuance, with Bloomberg reporting that the total debt raise would be between $20 billion and $25 billion. Meta did not immediately respond to a MarketWatch request for comment.

This comes after Meta raised $30 billion in debt last October - the company's largest-ever issuance. Credit-research company CreditSights reiterated its underperform rating on Meta's bonds on Thursday, saying that the price of Meta's bonds might drop because the company is flooding the market with debt.

See more: Amazon joins Meta, Google in jumbo bond club with up to $42 billion issuance

Anmuth was more critical of Meta's spending plans, downgrading the stock to neutral from overweight on Thursday. While Anmuth was impressed by Meta's 33% revenue growth this quarter, "we believe full-stack AI competition is intensifying and Meta has a more challenging path to returns on heavy AI capex beyond advertising," he wrote. Unlike the other three hyperscalers, Meta doesn't have a public cloud platform to rent out its infrastructure. That means Meta's AI investments must be monetized internally.

Hannah Pedone contributed.

-Christine Ji

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  04-30-26 1104ET

data.title: Caterpillar Lifts Outlook as AI-Fueled Power Demand Persists — Update
data.description:
By Connor Hart 




  Caterpillar said demand for its construction equipment, engines and generators remains strong despite rising geopolitical tensions and higher energy prices. 



  The company, known for its giant yellow dump trucks, bulldozers and excavators, is closely monitoring macroeconomic conditions in case recent pressures begin to weigh on demand, Chief Executive Joe Creed said on a call with analysts Thursday. 



  As of now, though, the added uncertainty hasn't been an issue. 



  Caterpillar raised its full-year outlook after reporting higher profit and sales in the latest quarter, helped in part by strong demand for large power-generation equipment used in data centers supporting artificial intelligence. 



  The AI boom is spurring additional construction activity, Creed said, providing a lift to the company's other business units. Caterpillar is also expecting some tariff relief, noting that costs tied to the levies this year won't be as steep as what was initially expected. 



  Shares were recently trading 9.2% higher, at $884.50, on track for a new all-time closing high. The rally extended the stock's year-to-date gain to more than 54%. 



  Sales across Caterpillar's power-and-energy division jumped 22% during the latest quarter. Growth is expected to continue in coming quarters, thanks to increasing energy demand to support data-center buildouts tied to cloud computing and generative AI, Creed said. 



  To meet surging demand, Caterpillar previously said it would spend $725 million at its Lafayette, Ind., plant to make more piston-driven engines for generators, marking its largest factory investment in about 15 years, The Wall Street Journal reported. Separately, it wants to more than double the production capacity for turbine engines by 2030. 



  "Notably, we're tracking ahead of our large-engine capacity expansion plans for the year," Creed said. 



  Sales across Caterpillar's construction-industries segment surged 38% in the recent quarter, while sales from the company's resource-industries division ticked 3.7% higher. Total sales and revenue for the period increased 22% to $17.42 billion and topped Wall Street models for $16.53 billion, according to FactSet. 



  The company said it now expects top-line growth in the low-double-digit percent range for the year. It previously guided for growth toward the high end of its long-term target for a 5%-to-7% compound annual growth rate. 



  Caterpillar additionally cut its outlook for tariff costs to a range of $2.2 billion to $2.4 billion in 2026, down from a prior forecast of about $2.6 billion. 



  First-quarter profit came in at $2.55 billion, or $5.47 a share, up from $2 billion, or $4.20 a share, a year earlier. Stripping out one-time items, adjusted earnings were $5.54 a share, ahead of analyst views for $4.65 a share. 





  Write to Connor Hart at connor.hart@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 30, 2026 10:59 ET (14:59 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: U.S. Leading Indicators Forecasts Further Slowdown
data.description:
By Jessica Coacci

U.S. leading economic indicators fell in March, signaling slower growth ahead as higher oil prices and supply-chain tensions will likely place additional upward pressure on inflation and further reduce consumers' purchasing power, according to a basket of monthly economic indicators.

The Leading Economic Index, or LEI, published Thursday by research group The Conference Board, declined by 0.6% in March to 97.3. That reversed its 0.3% increase in February to 97.9.

"After rising in February, the US LEI pulled back sharply in March, as building permits declined and consumer expectations and stock prices weakened," said Justyna Zabinska-La Monica, a senior manager at The Conference Board.

Overall, the LEI fell by 1% over the six months between September 2025 and March 2026, more than halving the rate of decline of its 2.1% contraction over the previous six-month period, the survey said.

The LEI is meant as a predictive index. It is based on 10 components, among them manufacturers' new orders, building permits for new private housing units, stock prices and consumer expectations, and aims to signal shifts in the business cycle.

The Conference Board released data for both February and March on Thursday due to delays tied to the U.S. federal government shutdown. The April report is scheduled for release on May 22.

Write to Jessica Coacci at jessica.coacci@wsj.com

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

April 30, 2026 10:47 ET (14:47 GMT)

Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Eurozone Economy Slows in First Quarter as Energy Shock Bites — 6th Update
data.description:
By Ed Frankl 




  The eurozone economy weakened at the start of the year, and is likely to struggle ahead as a jump in energy prices curbs consumer spending and delays a hoped-for recovery in industry. 



  The conflict in the Middle East is the latest blow to Europe's hopes for a sustained revival in its economic fortunes, after Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022 and President Trump's tariff blitz last year. 



  "The economy looked all set for a gradual recovery this year. The war in the Middle East has changed this. The adverse impact on the eurozone will only increase," said Carsten Brzeski, global head of macro for ING Research. 



  Gross domestic product in the eurozone grew 0.1% in the first quarter, down from 0.2% growth in the final three months of 2025, European Union statistics agency Eurostat said Thursday. A consensus of economists polled by The Wall Street Journal expected 0.2% growth. 



  Among member states, there were contrasting results. Germany's economy accelerated on a pickup in household and government spending. But in France, GDP flatlined on retreating private consumption and declining exports, while growth slowed in Italy, Spain, and the Netherlands, data showed. 



  As a net importer of energy, Europe is more exposed to the surging energy prices driven by conflict in the Middle East than the U.S. The European Union has spent an extra 27 billion euros, or around $31.53 billion, on energy imports due to higher prices since the first strikes on Iran at the end of February, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said Wednesday. 



  The European Central Bank, which held its key rate at 2.0% on Thursday, last month cut its forecast for eurozone growth this year to 0.9% from the 1.2% it predicted in December, and trimmed its forecast for 2027. 



  But the slowdown is likely to be more severe if the Strait of Hormuz remains closed to shipping for much of what remains of the year. Brent crude oil this week climbed above $120 a barrel, reflecting skepticism about the prospects for an early resumption of oil and natural gas flows through the choke point. 



  ECB President Christine Lagarde said at the bank's press conference Thursday that the eurozone is moving away from the bank's baseline toward a more adverse scenario stenciled last month that predicted a weaker 0.6% GDP growth this year. An even more severe scenario envisaged 0.4% growth. But she balked at suggestions that the economy was stagnating. 



  "It's lower growth, granted...but we're not in stagnation let alone in recession," she told reporters. "You can imagine scenarios where we're heading toward those situations. But this is not what we're seeing for the moment." 



  Surveys show Europeans are already feeling more fearful about the future. Consumer confidence in the eurozone sank to its lowest since December 2022, a time when inflation in the currency area was coming off double-digit highs. 



  Business confidence has also worsened. Germany's closely watched Ifo business-climate index nosedived to its lowest level since May 2020, when businesses were being hammered by lockdowns and restrictions as the Covid-19 pandemic spread globally. Surveys of purchasing managers in the eurozone showed activity at its weakest in 17 months in April, with output contracting and input and selling prices rising at their sharpest rates in more than three years. However, the eurozone's job market remained strong as the first quarter drew to a close, with the unemployment rate equaling a record low at 6.2% in March. 



  A fresh headwind might come in the form of higher interest rates. While the ECB has yet to respond to the pickup in inflation, investors expect it to act soon if there isn't any sign of a cooling in energy prices. 



  Figures also released by Eurostat Thursday show inflation in the eurozone rose to 3.0% in April from 2.6% in March, hitting its highest level since September 2023. 



  ECB policymakers will be gauging to what extent energy prices feed into more underlying price pressures in the economy, such as via demands for higher wages, which could lead to an inflationary spiral. However, they could also be attentive to stagflationary concerns, should high inflation be paired with low economic growth. 



  However, Lagarde said Thursday she would rather "park" that term in the 1970s, a time of more sustained inflation and high unemployment. 



  "We don't apply 'stagflation', that flashy term, to the circumstances that we have because we really think that it's associated with the 70s," she said. 





  Write to Ed Frankl at edward.frankl@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  April 30, 2026 10:48 ET (14:48 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
